speaker: interviewer
text: Hello, this is tape number 2 of an interview with Bob Geminder. And this is September the 24th, 1995. And Bob, when we finished with the last tape, you were telling us just where we were, with your grandmother arriving into the area where you were.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was actually talking more about the ghetto now. The ghetto was a small area-- a very, very small area-- in the city. Very, very high fences were put up. And the Jews were put into this ghetto. And the idea was that, you know, you-- the Jews at this point would become a little bit of a workforce for the Germans, out of this ghetto. For example, my mother would go every day, as part of a group of women and be let out of the ghetto in a group. And they would go clean apartments, clean toilets, clean houses, do that sort of thing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What, again, to go back to the family, it was my mother, her brother, my grandmother [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that were in the ghetto. And a very good friend of my mother's, Lola and Miriam Indik, were also there. And you'll see they played a very important role in, in, in my life, too. So that's why I'm mentioning them. And I don't know if I mentioned, my mother's brother was in the ghetto, too. And it's in this ghetto that my mother met my stepfather-- who's now my stepfather. His name was Emil Brotfeld. And he helped my mother get a place to live. And he was living, like, right below us. We were up on the-- I think it was, like, the third floor. And he was down on the first floor, in this apartment in the ghetto. We basically had, like, one room for my grandmother, my mother, and George and I. And we lived, we lived there during, during this ghetto--

speaker: interviewer
text: Ghetto period.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Period, right. Anyway, getting back to the-- my mother would go out and work. And it was, it was obvious to, I guess, everyone. I mean, even-- I remember seeing people on the street, dead. I remember Germans shooting people in the ghetto, just when they felt like it. It was very dangerous to be a child, at that point, too. Because believe it or not, the Germans would you know, be able to pick up children and just kill them brutally, to put it very bluntly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, just one incident that kind of stands out very much in the ghetto was the Germans would go search apartments. And many cases, when they searched apartments, they were looking for people that are not working, you know, that maybe are hiding and not going out to work. So I remember my grandmother knew-- saw them coming, knew they were coming. She put us in a cupboard, closed the door to this cupboard, of course told my brother and I to be very, very quiet. And somehow-- you know, it just amazes me, even today. You know, I was seven years old, but when I was told to be quiet, I guess the fear was so strong that I was quiet. And this was kind of throughout the war. I always wondered, as my own children were growing up and I couldn't keep them quiet, I said, how is it possible that I was able to do this? But I guess it was the fear.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, she put us in this cupboard. And she was smart enough-- and this was the second time my grandmother saved my life. She was smart enough to place wood in front of the door of this cupboard. She piled up a whole bunch of wood, in front of this cupboard. Because she knew that the Germans when they came like that, they always brought dogs with them. And sure enough, the Germans came up with the dogs. And they sniffed and sniffed. And the wood had such a strong smell that there was no way they could smell that there were a couple of little kids in this cupboard. So they left. Of course, we got out. The only other thing I remember in the ghetto was that I was always hungry. I mean, there was never enough food. My mother being-- worked on the outside, and she would sneak food in. You know, they snuck food in-- put their food in the brassieres, put the food wherever you could think of, you couldn't possibly imagine-- and brought some food home every night. Something, a piece of bread or something. Because there was just no food. And the daily killings would go on and go on and go on. So it was obvious that we had to do something drastic. So my mother tells the story, and I, of course, I wasn't there. But she went to the rabbi-- there was a rabbi in Stanislawow, in the ghetto-- and said, what should she do, because she has her mother and does she-- and the rabbi's answer was, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you must save the children. That's your number one priority. Save the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if you escape, your mother becomes secondary, it's OK. Because, she-- my mother just couldn't leave her mother. But the rabbi assured her that that was the proper thing to do. You know, even-- I guess, even to this day, when there's a question in this house, here, today-- like, what should we do? I always tell my wife to go ask the rabbi. Maybe it comes from that, from that scenario in Stanislawow. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So we made plans to escape. And my, my stepfather-- incidentally, we called him Wujek. 'Wujek' is the Polish name-- equivalent-- meaning for 'uncle.' So I could never call him Dad, because my father-- obviously he wasn't my father. But we called him Wujek. And even today, we still call him Wujek, which in Polish means 'uncle.' So anyway, at this point, of course, they were together, Wujek and my mother. And he had a sister in Warsaw that was, that had married a Gentile. And through a contact with her, he was able to get some papers-- some non-Jewish or gentile papers drawn up under a false name. And so arrangements were made that on the outside, if we were able to get out, we at least had some papers that we could then use and possibly try to escape. So the way we escaped was-- and I do remember this, myself-- that Lola and Miriam-- I told you that this lady Lola was, like, my mother's best friend, not a relative, but a friend. In fact, she just died in this country, about six months ago, at the age of 92.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lola and Miriam worked with my mother on this shift. So my mother took some things with her. And I was under Miriam's coat. This was evidently approaching winter, and it was cold. And my brother was under Lola's coat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, as they walked out of the ghetto, we were both being hidden by their coats and by the masses of people, as we were leaving the ghetto. And they took us to the place where they worked, this apartment. And, again, I guess I should be-- at this point, was getting used to staying in closets for a long time. They put me in a closet and my brother in a closet and said you must stay here and must be quiet till evening.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, in the evening-- they worked all day. Then, in the evening, instead of my mother going back to the ghetto, she didn't. She took us out of the closet, and, and we went. And we had a lady meet us, actually, who had these gentile papers. And we-- that's how we escaped from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name that you used on these papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Kaminska. Our name during the war was-- and I was able to use 'Robert,' because Robert is, is a name that's used in Poland. Robert Kaminska. And my brother was Jerzyk, which is the equivalent of George Kaminska. My mother was Berta Kaminska. And so with these papers, then, we went on a train. Actually, we were going towards Warsaw, Warszawa, to go to Wujek's sister, the person, the person that arranged the papers for us, the gentile papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he with you, at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was with us. There were the four of us, then, that escaped from the ghetto. But we decided, or they decided, that we're not going to stay together, that he would be alone. And my mother and the two children would be alone. And we'd try to somehow get to Warsaw that way, through various trains that we had to take. And there were several episodes. They don't really relate. I mean, they relate to me, but they, it's not something that I recall. Like, Wujek was taken and-- you know, away. And he had to buy his way out. So there was a, it was a, it was not an uneventful trip. But basically, for me, it was somewhat uneventful, as we went from Stanislawow to Warsaw, by train. And there we were met by Wujek's sister. And her name is Szutka. And her husband's name was Tadeus. And their name was Kuzhminska. My name-- our name was Kaminska, their name was Kuzhminska. And that was their real name. And nobody knew that she was Jewish, because they had been married for such a long time, so that nobody even suspected they were Jewish. In fact, she, of course, you know, she and he survived the war and had a daughter and so on. And, anyway, so that's how we got to Warszawa, to her apartment. We basically went to her apartment in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we stayed there for a while. We knew that couldn't work. So that, let me explain one other thing, too. In-- women didn't have much of a problem in being Gentiles in Europe. But a men had a problem. Because all males were-- all Jews were circumcised, and all non-Jews were not circumcised. So we were of course, were circumcised. So the males always had a problem. And I remember, like, the first day, almost the next day-- at least I'm told it was the next day-- when we got to Warsaw, I got very, very sick with typhus. And, you know, typhus has to be treated in a hospital. And we knew if I went to the hospital, they would immediately know I was Jewish. Again, because of this circumcision--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --thing. So I, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they made arrangements, they called a doctor, and so on. Basically, what the doctors kind of closed their eyes. They knew I was Jewish. The nurses knew I was Jewish. But nobody said a word. I remember my mother kind of trying to brainwash me, at that time, you know. You must speak Polish. Your name is Robert Kaminska. You are not a Geminder anymore. You're not, you know, and this, and on and on and on, through the whole night in the hospital. And then, of course, she had to leave me there, so I had to recover. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The nurses knew, of course, that I was Jewish. There was no question about it. Because of, again, what I said earlier. But I was, I guess, one of these kids that-- I remember running around the hospital when I got a little better and helping the nurses and helping them serve people. You know, I was like a little helper. And maybe that helped. You know, maybe my, my funny--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your attitude.

speaker: interviewee
text: My attitude at that time helped. But anyway, I got over the typhus and got out of the hospital. And you asked, Bernie, if we lived,

speaker: interviewer
text: With her sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: --With my, I guess I can call her my aunt-- Szutka. No, we stayed there for a little while then realized that can't possibly work. So we went ahead and tried to find some place to live, some apartment. This was a period that, of course, is really confusing in my head. I lost some of these early stages. Because we lived in the woods, we lived in apartments, we lived-- and this went on for a while. We tried things, crazy things, like even gluing my foreskin, so that it would look like I wasn't circumcised. We moved, like, every few days or every few weeks. Because somebody was always pointing a finger. And, in many cases, not so much at me, because I don't look Jewish, so to say. My brother was much, much darker, very dark hair and very dark eyes. And in Poland when a kid was that dark-looking, they immediately assumed he was Jewish. So he was kind of a dead giveaway for that. Now in some cases, too, I must say that the Polish people helped us, in many ways. There were many, many situations where the Polak-- Polish people would actually not turn us in. And they would come and warn us that the Germans are coming, that they're suspicious, and so on. They knew that at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was most of the hiding within the area of Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was, at that time, within the area of Warsaw. They, the other-- my mother also, at this point, things were very difficult. You know, there was no food, there was no place to stay, there was no money. The diamonds and gold were gone. They had been all used up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother decided that, again, listening to the rabbi, she figured out that she must save the children, George and me. So she found, through an old acquaintance near the city of Krakow, a person that she had known before the war. She called him an 'engineer.' I don't know if he really was an engineer, perhaps he was.

speaker: interviewee
text: She somehow located him and asked him to see if he could find a place where they could hide us. And he identified a woman that lived near, near Krakow, in a small village.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now was he Polish, or Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish, this was all Polish. So he identified a woman in Wieliczka, which is outside of Krakow, that said yes, she would do it. And basically what-- the deal was made. My mother made kind of a business deal with her. You save-- for each child, when you save him, you will have an apartment building. You know, I remember I mentioned my father had apartments in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Properties.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she actually signed an official piece of paper. This was around 1943, at this point, just to give you a time frame. And that if they saved me, they would have the property in Katowice. If they saved George, they would have the property in Bielsko.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, anyway, we were taken to this place, this farm. And it turned out to be like a farm with, you know, pigs and cows. And I remember milking cows there and drinking the milk right from the cow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And actually there was-- we, for the first time in a long time, I had, i had food. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Her name was Grukhalowa, this lady's name. It was the Grukhalowa family that kept us during this-- for to start with. And unfortunately there was another incident that occurred. We, of course, kind of were their cousins. And we stayed at the house. And so when they went to church on Sunday, we went to church on Sunday. We went to church on, on Sunday with them, every Sunday. And incidentally, the, the priest in that church near Krakow was, who's now the pope, he was the priest at that time. But anyway, we once went to the church. And lord and behold, my brother George, instead of taking his hat off, put his hat on. You know, some Jewish training came back to him. So immediately this started people buzzing that, you know, there was-- no Gentile Polish kid would ever do that. I mean, this was-- you were born and not know that. Well, in his case he made a terrible mistake. So it was immediately he must be Jewish, he must be this. So this lady Grukhalowa sent a letter or a telegram, whatever she sent or someone to tell my mother she must pick up the kids. It's not going to work. Because, in those days, if you were hiding Jews, you were putting yourself on the line for death.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: If the Germans found out that we were Jews, and this Grukhalowa family was hiding us, it would be the end of it, for them, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother came and picked up George. And this Grukhalowa lady decided that the only way to-- because my mother could not travel with two kids. She felt it was the danger. With one, she could somehow do it. So she left me there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Grukhalowa lady decided to hide me. So she hid me in the attic of this house that she was in. And I remember the steps to the attic, and I remember the attic, it was just an attic with hay and, and many days, unfortunately, they kind of forgot that I was there. And they forgot to feed me. I would, there was a chicken there that would lay an egg every once in a while. So I would be eating a raw egg, every once in a while. And then I was able, at night, for food, I was able to crawl out of the window of this attic. And there was a pear tree that kind of reached the roof line. And I was able to get-- grab some pears from the tree, when they were ripe, I suppose. I also was able to go down the steps, during the middle of the night. And right below the-- in one part of the attic there, there was, like, an area where they fed the animals. And I remember eating some of the stuff that the pigs had left, just to keep myself, just to eat. And I was on that attic for about three, four months. Until finally my mother was able to come back. As you can imagine, traveling was extremely difficult in those days, almost impossible, and Warsaw to Krakow was a long haul. I mean, you had to take some kind of a train, in those days. So she finally came back for me, to pick me up. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] And, to go on, I guess my mother tells a story. When she found me, she really thought I was already insane. She said I was talking to myself, and I was talking to my shadow. And I was covered with lice by the millions. And, but that's the way it was, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she, she took me back. She took me back to, to where they were living, which was near, near Warsaw, near Warszawa. Was it in a city, was it a country area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Country area. We mostly stayed, like, in the country areas. There were less police, there were less Germans. And you could, you could hide easier. In a large city, it was difficult. In fact, we started-- you know, this is, again, like, 1943. In order to have some money, to buy food or get something, we started making cigarettes. George and I were making cigarettes. My mother was somehow able to buy tobacco. And George and I would sit all day and stuff tobacco in a, in a little filter. It was like a-- in Poland, in those days, they smoked cigarettes, it was, like a half a filter and a half cigarette.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we would stuff the tobacco into this other half, with the paper, and kind of burn the end a little bit with a match and cut it off, so the tobacco wouldn't fall out. And, you know, when you do something for 10 hours a day, like George and I, we made hundreds of cigarettes. I don't know, maybe even thousands. Not per day, but whatever. And then my mother was able to go out and sell these cigarettes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everything my mother did-- again, because, going back to the woman, and my mother was a redhead, you know, so she blended right in to the Polish environment. It wasn't like, she didn't, so to say, look Jewish. And obviously, with a woman they couldn't check if she was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she would go out and sell these cigarettes. And that's how we made, you know, some money to-- Make ends meet.

speaker: interviewee
text: --To support ourselves or to, to get food. But many times, you know, there was no tobacco, we couldn't make them. I remember my-- Wujek and I and George would go out in the fields and try to steal potatoes from farms, try to steal food. And many times we would, they would find out. And we'd, again, have to move. So it was a constant plight, you might say, moving, and so on. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One story I'd like to relate, which is a little off, off the path. But I've told this story to my kids so many times, they would be angry if I didn't tell it. But in all of this mess, we once went on an outing, you know, a Sunday outing, and, the four of us. And I brought-- I always liked to catch things, like whether it was worms or butterflies or what have you. So I brought a little jar with me. And, and I found there was a little pond, there, where the little guppies were-- all kinds of little fish, little guppies, maybe half-inch size or something like that. So I started collecting these little guppies in my jar. And I collected them, like for all day, collected fish. It was a lot of fun. And finally we, we decided we were ready to go home, it was getting dark.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm walking home. And I stumbled on something, and the jar fell. And all the fish spilled out. And I yelled out, [POLISH], which, translated means 'the fish got spilled.' And I guess the reason it's such a funny story because, even today, when Mindy or Ellen-- my kids-- or my wife drop something, I say [POLISH], meaning that they dropped something. It's [POLISH], you know? That one word still catch-- stays with us. The fish got spilled, you know. So we basically, you know, went, went through this time frame, you know, escaping, hiding. I remember being in a house where when my-- sometimes Wujek would go with my mother, at least part of the way, to help her buy or sell the cigarettes or do something, or find food. And he would leave us alone in this one room.

speaker: interviewee
text: And most of those times, you know, the bathrooms weren't part of the house. The bathroom was maybe 100 yards away, an outhouse. And we were asked to-- when they left, they said, you can't go to the bathroom. You're not allowed to go the bathroom, because you'll be seen, and George will be seen. So there we were, like, spending all day in a, in this apartment, without even being able to go to the bathroom. And so George and I, of course, were very, very close. I mean, we spent, you know, 24 hours a day, you know, there was no school, there was no, no diversions of any sort. It was just the two of us together, always, in, during this time frame in the war. And, anyway, just to go on, there was a, I know I'm not giving you any chance to ask questions, Bernie.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's all right. Go right ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] To go on, there was a time, you know, there was a time frame where all of a sudden, we started hearing gunshots and cannons and airplanes. And, of course, for us this was good news, because we-- there was no such thing as a newspaper that would tell you what's going on in the war. We had no idea what was going on at that time. This-- at least at this point. And we heard this. And we got very nervous about it, because there were a lot of rumors the Russians were coming, Russians were coming. And we were afraid that if the Russians came into these small villages, then we just didn't know what would happen. So we decided to go to Warsaw, to Szutka, Wujek's sister's, house, and go there and see if we could spend the time there. We, we felt at this point, it might be safer to be in a big city, OK. Big mistake, it turned out. But anyway, that's what we decided. Because while we went to the city-- this was now, again, 1944, to give a time frame-- the uprising started, the Polish Warsaw uprising. This was the situation where the Polish underground-- you know, there was always an underground in, in Poland. There was, it was made up of Polish people. It was made up of a lot of Jews. And in Warsaw, of course, the underground was very strong. Every once in a while, you'd hear that there was a big explosion at a German headquarters, because the underground did something. A train was derailed, or something like this. Just to back up a bit, when we first got to Warsaw, you know, the Warsaw ghetto fire. They burned the Warsaw ghetto. And I remember we were, like, half a mile or a quarter of a mile from the ghetto. We actually saw the flames and saw the people. And I didn't see this, but my mother said she saw people crawling out of sewers, Jews crawling out of sewers, that somehow escaped this fire. But a lot of these-- the point I'm making-- a lot of these Jews then went into the woods and hid and became part of the underground, the resistance movement. So in 1944, as the Russians were approaching towards Warsaw, the Germans decided-- the Polish underground, I should say, decided to rise up against the Germans, to capture their own capital, to capture their city.
